coredata.dc:title: Sequential processing of nitrogen-rich, biowaste-derived carbon quantum dots combined with strontium cobaltite for enhanced supercapacitive performance
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Rita
Kharangarh, Poonam R.
Singh, Vinamrita
Jha, Ranjana
Ravi Kant, Chhaya
coredata.dc:description: Disposal of waste edible oil is a major concern for government regulatory bodies worldwide, with improper disposal leading to the blocking of sewer lines, polluting water bodies, and degrading the environment. In the present work, waste soybean oil is converted into soot and hydrothermally processed to produce nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The N-CQDs are further treated with strontium cobaltite (SC) prepared via the sol-gel method. A significant difference is observed in the morphology, surface area, and electrochemical performance of the N-CQDs (124 Fg−1), SC particles (106.7 Fg−1), and the combined SC@N-CQDs material (386.5 Fg−1). The bio derived SC@N-CQD sample is used as an electrode in a symmetrical supercapacitor, and the device shows EDLC behavior with co-contribution of minor pseudocapacitance due to the nitrogen functional groups and Co2+/Co3+ states of the SC. The SC@N-CQDs deliver an exceptionally high specific capacitance of 180.24 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1 due to the enhanced surface area offered by the combined material, good conductivity imparted by the N-CQDs, and oxygen vacancies in the SC. The synergy leads to fast ion diffusion between the electrode and the electrolyte interface, and an additional boost is achieved by the pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N present in the electrode material. The capacitance retention is 80.8% of the initial capacitance after 5000 cycles. The SC@N-CQD supercapacitor provides a high energy density of 7.51 Whkg−1 and can operate a red LED, thus demonstrating the efficient utilization of waste edible oils to yield high-performance material for supercapacitor applications. This study can have a broad environmental impact on the reduction and reuse of biowaste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste edible oil
Soot
Symmetric supercapacitor
Strontium cobaltite
Carbon quantum dot

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author (Rita Kumari) would like to express her sincere gratitude to Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women (IGDTUW) , Delhi for providing the university Junior Research Fellowship for smooth ongoing research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics representing the synthesis of N-CQDs, strontium cobaltite (SC), and SC@N-CQDs.
FESEM images along with particle size distribution and elemental composition of (a) N-CQDs, (b) SC, and (c) SC@N-CQDs.
HRTEM images and SAED pattern of (a) N-CQDs and (b) SC@N-CQDs samples.
XRD measurements of N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs samples.
FTIR spectra of N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs samples.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) BJH pore diameter distributions for the samples.
Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of (a) C 1 s for N-CQDs, (b) C 1 s for SC@N-CQDs, and (c) Co 2p for SC.
Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of N 1 s for (a) N-CQDs, (b) SC, and (c) SC@N-CQDs.
Cyclic Voltammetry measurements of (a) N-CQDs, (b) SC, and (c) SC@N-CQDs.
(a) GCD cycles, (b) capacitance contribution, and (c) Nyquist plots of the samples.
Cyclic Voltammetry measurements (a) at varying voltage windows, (b) at varying scan rates, and (c) GCD curve for SC@N-CQDs two electrode device.

(a) Impedance measurements (Nyquist plots) at different cycles and (b) Coulombic efficiency and capacitance retention for the symmetrical two-electrode SC@N-CQDs device. The inset shows three supercapacitor devices connected in series to power an LED.
Schematics representing the charge storage mechanism in the symmetric SC@N-CQDs based supercapacitor.
Crystallite and physisorption characteristics of the prepared samples.
Comparison table of CQDs and their composites along with the key parameters of the SC@N-CQD supercapacitor fabricated for the present work.

head.ce:title: Sequential processing of nitrogen-rich, biowaste-derived carbon quantum dots combined with strontium cobaltite for enhanced supercapacitive performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women, Kashmere Gate, Delhi 110006, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women, Kashmere Gate, Delhi- 110006, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, East Campus, Delhi 110031, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, East Campus, Delhi,110031, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, Dwarka, New Delhi 110078, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, Dwarka, New Delhi- 110078, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disposal of waste edible oil is a major concern for government regulatory bodies worldwide, with improper disposal leading to the blocking of sewer lines, polluting water bodies, and degrading the environment. In the present work, waste soybean oil is converted into soot and hydrothermally processed to produce nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The N-CQDs are further treated with strontium cobaltite (SC) prepared via the sol-gel method. A significant difference is observed in the morphology, surface area, and electrochemical performance of the N-CQDs (124 Fg−1), SC particles (106.7 Fg−1), and the combined SC@N-CQDs material (386.5 Fg−1). The bio derived SC@N-CQD sample is used as an electrode in a symmetrical supercapacitor, and the device shows EDLC behavior with co-contribution of minor pseudocapacitance due to the nitrogen functional groups and Co2+/Co3+ states of the SC. The SC@N-CQDs deliver an exceptionally high specific capacitance of 180.24 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1 due to the enhanced surface area offered by the combined material, good conductivity imparted by the N-CQDs, and oxygen vacancies in the SC. The synergy leads to fast ion diffusion between the electrode and the electrolyte interface, and an additional boost is achieved by the pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N present in the electrode material. The capacitance retention is 80.8% of the initial capacitance after 5000 cycles. The SC@N-CQD supercapacitor provides a high energy density of 7.51 Whkg−1 and can operate a red LED, thus demonstrating the efficient utilization of waste edible oils to yield high-performance material for supercapacitor applications. This study can have a broad environmental impact on the reduction and reuse of biowaste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Scientists have been motivated to search for sustainable fuels due to steadily depleting fossil fuel sources and the rise in atmospheric pollutants. Increasing demand for energy led to an incessant use of fossil fuels, posing a great threat to the environment. To address these pressing concerns, we need highly efficient, affordable, clean, and environmentally safe energy storage devices with capabilities of simultaneously offering high power density and energy density as well as long cycle life. The future of energy storage technology revolves around batteries, supercapacitors, and hybrid technologies having high power densities, flexibility, and optimal cyclability [1–4]. Supercapacitor devices received much focus due to their high power density and prolonged cycle life in comparison to batteries [5–9]. However, there are still many challenges with deploying supercapacitors commercially because of their limited energy density and capacitance achieved so far. To overcome these limitations, there is a need to explore suitable materials that exhibit more charge accumulation and good cycling stability. Carbon-based materials are suitable for energy storage due to their porosity, high surface area, less resistive nature, and demonstration of electric double layer capacitance (EDLC) behavior.

In recent times, material advancements led to many reports on high-performance, intercalation-type supercapacitor electrodes with increased power density and retention [10]. The mechanism of intercalation of large-sized positive and small-sized negative charges still needs to be explained due to their discriminating size difference [11]. Perovskite-type oxides (A2BO4 or ABO3; A is an alkali element/lanthanide earth metal and B is a transition metal) recently gained interest for supercapacitor application owing to their combined electronic and ionic conductivity at high temperatures, greater rate capability, and increased capacitance [12–14]. The optimum closed formula for perovskite oxides, which have a cubic or nearly cubic structure, is ABO3. The A and B sites in the perovskite structure may accommodate ions of various sizes and valences, demonstrating remarkable diversification in these compounds. Each B site cation forms an octagonal (BO6) bond with 6 oxygen atoms in the cubic structure, while each A site cation forms a stable perovskite structure with 12 oxygen atoms [15]. Practically, BO6 component in the perovskite's structure is considered the primary regulator of its electrochemical capabilities. Substitution of another cation with a different radius and valence at the A and B sites varies the physicochemical properties of the perovskite [16,17]. The ionic and electronic conductivity of perovskites can be tailored with ease giving enough motivation to examine them as supercapacitor electrodes [18].

Recent research has shown that strontium-based bimetallic oxides (SrxMyOz; M=metal ion) have good metallic characteristics and have a huge potential for use in supercapacitor applications [8]. Xiao et al. [19] in 2005 reported the synthesis and electrochemical studies of the Brown-Millerite SrCoO2.5 as electrodes for supercapacitor. By doping strontium cobaltite (SrCoO) oxides with cations or anions that have a higher oxidation state, one can compensate for the loss of oxygen at higher temperatures. Zhu et al. [20] synthesized an efficient electrode SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ for use in supercapacitor. Their findings demonstrate that the storage capacitance in this type of perovskite oxide is 773.6 at 0.5 Ag−1 and exceeds 96% at a discharge rate of 10 Ag−1. However, the low intrinsic conductivity, a basic fault of perovskite oxides, prevents them from being used in advanced energy applications. Therefore, several works investigated combining carbonaceous materials with perovskites to compensate for the low conductivity. A variety of carbon materials, such as activated carbon [21], carbon nanotubes [22], graphene and its oxide [23–25], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [26–28], are utilized in supercapacitors either as lone electrode material or in combination with other metal-based materials. Apart from the unique properties of CQDs brought on by strong quantum specific confinement and edge effects [29,30], these materials can be used as a conductive electrical component for improving electrochemical behavior [30–32]. Kharangarh et al. [33] synthesized Nickel Cobaltite/ Graphene QD (NiCo2O4/GQDs) composites that served as improved electrode material for supercapacitors. Arsalani et al. [34] prepared carbon quantum dots/CoS2 nanocomposite (CQDs/CoS2) using solvothermal method and reported high specific capacitance of 808 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1 and an excellent retention rate of 98.75% after 10000 cycles.

Considering the effectiveness of perovskite and carbonaceous materials to synergize together, this work uniquely combines CQDs obtained from waste soybean oil-derived soot with strontium cobaltite synthesized using sol-gel method. A commercially attractive sequential hydrothermal process is deployed to obtain the combined material, namely SC@N-CQDs. Remarkably, the CQDs are rich in nitrogen, which is previously shown to help attain good capacitance [35–37]. As anticipated, excellent electrochemical performance is projected by SC@N-CQDs, which has a lot of potential for supercapacitor applications. Thus, the environment-friendly (green approach) synthesis along with utilizing waste oil facilitates reusing and recycling of biowaste, reduces cost implication, and provides an attractive method of developing efficient electrode material.

Waste soybean oil (Fortune Soya Health, purity ∼98%), ammonia solution (purity ∼99%), NaPF6 sodium hexafluorophoshate (purity ∼98%), ethylene carbonate (C3H4O3) (purity ∼99%), propylene carbonate (C4H6O3) (purity 99%), Co(NO3)2·6 H2O cobalt(ous) nitrate hexahydrate (purity ∼99%), Sr(NO3)2 strontium nitrate (purity ∼99%), carbon black (purity 99 +% metals basis), PVDF-HFP poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexa-fluoropropylene) (purity ∼ 99%), graphite sheets from Nanoshel, and 1-methyl-2-Pyrrollidone (NMP) (C5H9NO) (purity >99%) are used in the present work. Reagent grade triple deionized water is used throughout the study.
The three steps for the synthesis of N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 and described below:

The first phase involves the formation of N-CQDs by placing 2.5 g soot in an autoclave at 150 °C for 8 h with 5 ml of ammonia and 175 ml of deionized (DI) water. To increase the pH value above 10, 0.1 g of potassium hydroxide (KOH) is added to the solution. 1.4 g of N-CQDs is obtained giving a yield of 56%. The second phase involves preparing the SC nanoparticles via the sol-gel method. 5.5 g of Sr(NO3)2 and 7.0 g of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O are mixed in 50 ml of DI water for 6 h at 80 °C using a magnetic stirrer, resulting in a dark brown solution. The solution is stirred for an additional 30 min at 90 °C to produce a viscous gel. The resulting gel is calcined for 8 h at 1100 °C to remove the organic impurities. In the third stage, the hydrothermal method is used to prepare SC@N-CQDs. 100 mg of SC and 50 mg of N-CQDs are mixed finely in a mortar and added to 180 ml of DI water. The solution is hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 8 h. The resultant mixture is washed multiple times in DI water and with ethanol, followed by drying at 60 °C in an oven overnight.

For the preparation of electrodes, 80 wt% of SC, N-CQDs, and SC@N-CQDs each, 10 wt% Super P, and 10 wt% PVDF are mixed in NMP solvent using mortar pestle. The obtained slurry is uniformly applied on a sheet of graphite (1″ × 1″) and allowed to dry overnight at 80 °C for three-electrode and two-electrode configurations. The three-electrode measurements are carried out with N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs-based electrodes as working electrodes, a platinum rod as a counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode in 0.5 M NaPF6. The performance testing of two-electrodes devices is done by forming a sandwich configuration with the SC@N-CQDs-based electrodes separated by an electrolyte (0.5 M NaPF6) dipped separator.

The characterization of all three samples and device testing are conducted under ambient conditions. The morphological studies of the synthesized materials are investigated using NOVA Nano FE-SEM 450 provided with energy dispersive analyzer (EDAX) for measuring different elemental compositions. X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical pert Powder) with Cu Kα radiation source and range of 2θ (10–80°) is used for structural analysis. The size distribution of pores and surface area is investigated by Quantachrome Nova LX2 (BET) analyser with nitrogen gas sorption at 77 K. Thermo fisher scientific XPS system with 400 µm X-ray spot size, Al-Kα source, and Ar ions flood gun to reduce the charge shift is used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR), IRAffinity-1S Shimadzu, is used to probe into the functional groups present in the samples in a range of 4000–400 cm−1. The samples are converted into pellets after mixing with KBr in ∼1:1000 ratio. The high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) images are acquired using Tecnai 200 KV TEM (Fei, Electron Optics) equipped with selected area electron diffraction (SAED). The electrochemical studies on potentiostat (Autolab) of the supercapacitor includes cyclic-voltagram (CV), impedance spectrometry (Nyquist), galvanostatic charging and discharging (GCD). For calculating the specific capacitance (CS), energy density (E), and power density (P), the formulae given in reference [36] are used.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images of the N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs material, along with their EDX patterns and size distribution histograms. N-CQDs clearly possess spherical geometry with uniform particle size averaging at ∼8 nm. The SC particles are irregularly shaped and agglomerated, and the average particle size is ∼2.25 µm. The formation of micrometer-sized particles in SC is due to diffusion-limited aggregation [11]. The fused particles are usually observed in sol-gel synthesized materials since the process involves nucleation and aggregation [38]. The competition between these two processes affects the final particle size along with other reaction conditions. In this case since water is used for the sol-gel synthesis of SC, the drying process leads to particle coalescing when the water evaporates and the particles concentrate together [39].

In contrast to the morphology of N-CQDs and SC particles, the SC@N-CQD have needle-shaped geometry decorated with nanodots. The microneedles are ∼3.2 µm long with a thickness of 85 nm and may have resulted when the micrometer scale flakes cracked into the shape of microneedles [40]. The abundant microneedles with nanodots provide favorable morphology with a high specific surface area leading to more accessible reactive sites. A stronger contact between the electrolyte and exposed active sites can be established, offering a fast transfer of electrons with several channels for ion diffusion [41]. From the EDX spectrum and elemental composition tabulated in the inset of Fig. 2, the samples are composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, strontium, and cobaltite without any other trace elements. The carbon content is maximum in N-CQDs, and it is rich in nitrogen. The SC has the highest amount of oxygen content, while an almost equal percentage of Sr and Co. The SC@N-CQDs possess elements of both materials.

The morphologies of N-CQDs and the composite material are further analyzed by HRTEM ( Fig. 3). The HRTEM images of the N-CQDs depict that the N-CQDs are circular in shape and have small thicknesses with sizes distributed in the nanometer range. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern displays diffused rings for N-CQDs due to the amorphous nature of the sample. The HRTEM images of the SC@N-CQDs clearly show the formation of nanoneedles having lengths in the range of 40–100 nm and an average thickness of ∼10 nm. The SAED pattern of the composite depicts a combination of diffused rings along with the bright spots corresponding to the poly-crystalline nature of strontium cobaltite.

The crystalline structure of N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs samples are determined using XRD measurements ( Fig. 4). The broad peak in N-CQDs for plane (002), identified at 2θ = 24.17°, results from the discontinuity in the stacking of graphitic carbon layers, while the (100) peak at 44.29° corresponds to the in-plane structure of the graphitic crystallites.

In Fig. 4, the existence of the diffraction peaks for (001), (101), (111), (110), (105), (200), (106), (113), (214), and (216) planes in the prepared SC sample shows the successful formation of perovskite structure with JCPDS card No. 48–0875 [42–44]. The SC material has a tetragonal structure as the main phase of the crystal. Here, Sr combines with the Co atom in octahedral coordination with six oxygen atoms [45]. Due to the disorder in active sites of oxygen atoms in the crystal structure, Sr atoms occupy the space in eight corner-sharing of the octahedral Co atom [34]. The XRD data further reveals that the primary phase of SC@N-CQDs is also a tetragonal structure equivalent to the Sr2CoO4.

The average crystallite size is computed using Debye–Scherrer formula
D =
K λ
β cos θ
, where wavelength, λ = 0.154 nm, Scherrer's constant, K = 0.9 or 0.98 for nearly spherical particles or rod-like particles, respectively [46,47], β = full width at half maximum (FWHM), and θ = Bragg's angle. Table 1 gives the values of the lattice constant a, interplanar spacing d, and strain (ε) [48,49]. The average crystallite size calculated for N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQD is 1.68, 22.04, and 10.93 nm, respectively. The SC and SC@N-CQDs possess a negative value of microstrain, which indicates compressive strain leading to lattice compression [50] and this directly correlates with the particle size as well [51]. An increase in compressive strain reduces the particle size of SC@N-CQDs.

From the FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 5, the vibrational bands of N-CQDs are observed at 1627, 2355, 2852, 2929, and 3448 cm−1, which relate to the carbonyl functional group of amide [N–CO], carboxylic group [CC] stretching, [C-N] stretching, C-H stretching of CH, CH2, and CH3, and -OH/N-H stretching, respectively [25,52]. These five peaks are common in all three samples with the presence of some additional bands. The additional bands in SC occur at 584 and 667 cm−1, while in SC@N-CQDs additional bands appear at 436, 584, 667, 850, 1462, and 1741 cm−1. The peaks at 584 and 667 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of Co-O and Sr-O. The transmittance wavenumber in the range 548–590 cm−1 supports the stretching vibrations for [Co(III)-O] bond of [CoO6] octahedron structure, and the stretching vibrations in the range 653–670 cm−1 is favorable for [Co(II)-O] bond in tetrahedron [CoO4] [34]. The weak transmittance peaks between 450 and 550 cm–1 result from the vibration of the Sr2+. The bonding of SrCO3 with CoO6 is confirmed from the bands visible between 400 and 500 cm−1, demonstrating the formation of SC and is consistent with the XRD data. Also, the peaks related to C-C near 1462 cm−1 confirm the linking of the N-CQDs with SC resulting in the enhancement of the absorption peak.

The nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherms of the prepared samples, Fig. 6(a), are used to estimate the specific surface area (SBET), pore diameter (Pdia), and pore volume (Pvol). The physisorption characteristics are listed in Table 1. Each sorption curve traces a type (IV) and H1 isotherm loop [53]. The formation of a hysteresis loop arises when capillary condensation occurs in mesoporous materials because the adsorption and desorption paths are not the same. The hysteresis loop type and pore shape are primarily connected [54]. Materials having an even distribution of cylindrical and spherical narrow mesopores show a hysteresis (H1) type loop. The SC@N-CQDs have the highest surface area (owing to the nanodot-coated microneedle morphology), followed by N-CQDs and SC. It is interesting to note that the N-CQDs have a narrow distribution of pore diameter with an average of 3.37 nm (Fig. 6(b)). On the other hand, SC has a very large pore diameter, as depicted by SEM micrographs as well, and this reduces the surface area of SC to a large extent. Such porosity is not beneficial for the application of charge storage and leads to very low capacitance values. Yet, looking at the pore size curve of SC@N-CQD, there is a wide distribution of pore diameters ranging from 2.5 to 50 nm and peaking at 5 nm and 28 nm. Thus, SC@N-CQD classifies as a mesoporous material, which is highly preferred in electrochemical devices.

The XPS spectra of all three samples are examined to understand the valance states with surface chemical composition. The full scan graphs, provided in Fig. S1 of supplementary information, verify the presence of C, O and N in N-CQDs; Sr, Co, O, and N in SC; and Sr, Co, C, O and N in SC@NCQDs. The oxygen content is highest (56%) in the SC sample, which may hamper the performance of the electrochemical electrode. The different binding states of the elements present in the samples are provided in Table S1. The high-resolution spectra of N-CQDs and SC@N-CQDs for carbon content, Fig. 7(a and b), reveal that sp2/sp3 ratio is ∼1.98% and ∼1.13%, respectively. Research studies report that the sp2/sp3 ratio plays a vital role in determining the conductivity of carbon electrodes, and higher ratio results in higher conductivity [55–57]. Thus, it is evident that the carbon is present in the form of sp2 more than sp3 in N-CQDs and SC@N-CQDs.

The XPS spectrum peaks of Sr in the SC sample at 133.1 eV and 134.8 eV correspond to Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2, respectively, as shown in Fig. S3(d). These peaks shift to 133.9 eV and 135.7 eV after hydrothermal treatment during SC@N-CQDs synthesis (Fig. S4 (e)). The XPS scan for Co in SC (Fig. 7(c)) displays one peak at 780.2 eV classified for Co 2p3/2, which can be deconvoluted to reveal Co2+ state at 781.4 eV and the Co3+ state at 780 eV. The Co 2p1/2 peak occurs at 796.8 eV in which the Co2+ peak is present at 781.2 eV and the Co3+ peak at 779.9 eV. The presence of two satellite peaks is also evident at 787.6 eV and 803.7 eV as marked in the figure [20,58].

From the XPS spectra of Co 2p, it is clear that the Co2+ content is more than the Co3+ content representing the reduction of Co3+ to Co2+, thereby creating oxygen vacancies. Oxygen vacancies result into good charge storage properties of the SC electrode. A further validation of the oxygen vacancies present in SC is obtained by deconvoluting the O 1 s spectrum of SC (Fig. S3 (a)), where two peaks corresponding to O2 −2/O- (highly oxidative oxygen) and -OH/O2 (adsorbed oxygen) are identified at 529.3 eV and 531.1 eV, respectively [58]. The latter oxygen peak is directly proportional to the density of the oxygen vacancies. In case of SC@N-CQDs, an additional peak of adsorbed H2O is also visible at a binding energy of 536.4 eV. The extra electrons present in the samples due to these oxygen vacancies provide conductivity to the SC-based electrode.

The functional form of nitrogen also affects the performance of electrochemical electrodes. From Fig. 8(a-c), the nitrogen is present in several different forms, i.e., for N-CQDs, pyridinic-N (N-6) and pyrrolic-N (N-5) peaks are detected, while for SC, peaks corresponding to only N-6 and oxidized-N are present. In the case of SC@NCQDs, the N-content is present in all three forms: N-6, N-5, and oxidized-N. The N-6 and N-5 have enhancing effects on the capacitive performance of the electrodes due to the presence of a positive charge, reduction of the charge transfer resistance, and increase in the wettability between the electrode and the electrolyte [37,59,60]. The oxidized-N is detrimental for the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors, and in this case, SC has 30.5% of oxidized-N content as compared to only 8.3% in SC@N-CQDs.

To evaluate the electrochemical performances of the synthesized samples, a standard three-electrode cell is assembled as described in the experimental section. The mass loadings on the working electrode for N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs are 9.6 × 10−4, 9.9 × 10−4, 9.7 × 10−4 g. The CV curves are acquired within a potential window of 0–1.0 V at varying scan rates. Interesting observations are made with respect to the charge storage mechanism involved in the three devices, which correlate well with the elemental composition and porosity of the materials. The CV curves for N-CQDs display EDLC behavior with hints of pseudocapacitance and have symmetric plots on anodic and cathodic sides ( Fig. 9(a)). The symmetry indicates a fast and reversible charging and discharging of the electrodes [61]. The deviation from the rectangular shape results from the nitrogen content of ∼14% in the CQD electrode [60]. Insignificant distortion in the CV plots is observed at high scan rates.

Contrarily, the CV plots of SC electrode are asymmetrical, yet display EDLC behavior without any faradic peaks (Fig. 9(b)). The asymmetry is more pronounced at higher scan rates. Such behavior reveals that non-uniform ionic adsorption/desorption is present in SC electrode material. This is commonly observed when the morphology is highly agglomerated with irregular particle shapes as observed in the SEM micrographs [62]. The fused particles of SC electrode provide a smaller number of exposed reactive sites due to the low surface area. In addition, the area under the curve of CV plots is directly proportional to the CS of the device [36]. In line with the BET measurements, the area of the CV plot at a fixed scan rate is more for N-CQDs device than SC device because of the larger surface area and porosity of N-CQDs leading to better penetration of the electrolyte.

The synergistic effect of combining SC and N-CQDs is observed in the CV plots of SC@N-CQDs (Fig. 9(c)). Firstly, the plots become symmetrical in the anodic and cathodic regions demonstrating the reversibility of the electrode at various scan rates. Secondly, the area of the CV plot is greatly enhanced as compared to the other two materials individually. This enhanced output of the SC@N-CQDs is further confirmed by the GCD curves, where it is observed that the time taken to discharge is much longer than the N-CQDs and SC electrodes at 0.5 Ag−1 ( Fig. 10(a)). Thus, in the three-electrode configuration, the specific capacitance values of N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs electrodes are 124.0, 106.7, and 386.5 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1, respectively.

The power law equation for current vs scanning rate given by i = av
b
[27] is used to further analyze the contribution of EDLC and pseudocapacitance towards the total capacitance [26,27]. In this equation, the value of b lies between 0.5 and 1, where b = 0.5 is indicative of diffusion-controlled process (pseudocapacitive) and b = 1 indicates surface controlled capacitance (EDLC). The b value can be extracted from the double logarithmic i-v graph. The segregated contribution of each component can be obtained using the current at a constant voltage v:
i
v
=
k
1
v +
k
2
v
1 / 2
where k
1 and k
2 are constants extractable from the CV curves at different scan rates [63]. As shown in Fig. 10(b), the N-CQDs demonstrate the highest amount of EDLC behavior (98.38%), which is reasonable for a carbon-based electrode. The SC electrode has 76.59% of EDLC contribution while SC@N-CQDs show 85.54% contribution from the EDLC mechanism. The results correspond well with the expectation because various functional groups of nitrogen and oxygen are present within the electrode materials.

The Nyquist plots (Fig. 10(c)) give an understanding of the charge transfer and storage mechanism of the three electrode materials. The internal resistance (Ri), given by the intercept of the curve on the real axis at high frequency region, is smallest for SC@NCQDs with a value of 20.93 Ω followed by N-CQDs and SC with a value of 27.45 and 34.27 Ω, respectively. This shows that the conductivity of the composite is enhanced leading to better charge storage. The ion diffusion resistance (RD) related to the mass transfer is found to be 2.60, 1.63, and 1.49 Ω for N-CQDs, SC, and SC@N-CQDs, respectively, which shows that the SC@N-CQDs has the least ion diffusion resistance because of its mesoporous nature. On analyzing the slope of the mid-frequency region, it is found that charging process in N-CQD electrode is strongly controlled by the EDL formation.

To establish the practical usage of the prepared material, SC@N-CQDs-based two-electrode device is assembled with ∼ 9.5 × 10−4 g active mass on each electrode. The CV plots at varying potential windows from 1.0 V to 1.6 V show distortion after 1.25 V and the asymmetry in the plots also increase ( Fig. 11(a)). The CV curves show the same trend as a three-electrode device, where the symmetry is maintained at different scan rates with a potential window of 1.0 V (Fig. 11(b)). The GCD plots at different current densities are triangular and imply that the supercapacitor is electrochemically reversible with good Coulombic efficiency (Fig. 11(c)). The CS calculated from the GCD curves are 180.24 and 88.9 Fg−1 for 0.5 and 4.0 Ag−1, respectively, thus demonstrating the good rate capability of the electrodes owing to the mesoporosity of the SC@N-CQDs material. The key parameters of the SC@N-CQD supercapacitor are provided in Table 2.

The presence of bulk effects in the cell is confirmed by one semicircular arc in the Nyquist plots ( Fig. 12(a) and its inset). The supercapacitor behaves nearly to an ideal capacitor when the impedance plot is nearly parallel to the imaginary axis. The electrode resistance (Re), Ri, RD, and polarization resistance (RP) are equal to 14.70, 18.08, 0.45, and 4.01 Ω, respectively. Further, the energy density is 7.51 Whkg−1 and the power density is 166.7 Wkg−1. To study the stability of the cell, GCD plots up to 5000 cycles are acquired, and it is found that 80.8% of the CS is retained at 1.2 Ag−1. This demonstrates the excellent stability of the electrode (Fig. 12(b)). Moreover, the Coulombic efficiency remains above 92.0%, while the Re and RP change by a small amount of ∼4 and 0.14 Ω only (Fig. 12(a)).

The above results can be summarized based on the properties of the produced materials. The morphology of the SC@N-CQDs is favorable for charge storage because the needle-like structure provides voids between the particles in which the electrolyte ions can penetrate deeply. The N-CQDs have small particle size but the spherical geometry makes the material more densely packed. The surface features of the particles directly relate to their surface area where it is observed that SC@N-CQDs has the highest surface area and wide distribution of pores, i.e., mesoporosity.

The addition of carbon material makes SC@N-CQDs more conductive and accelerates the transfer of charges, especially because sp2 >sp3. Further, the XPS analysis shows that SC is rich in oxygen vacancies that impart conductivity to it. The synergism of mixing SC and N-CQDs has led to an increased conductivity of SC@N-CQDs, which is confirmed by the Ri of the three electrodes. The bulk phase of the SC also gives powerful assistance in providing greater capacity to the electrodes for energy storage.

The presence of N and O functionalities is known to increase the electrical conductivity and contribute towards the pseudocapacitance [27,37,60]. These electrochemically active heteroatoms generate extra capacitance through reversible Faradaic reactions [23,26]. This may be the reason for the quasi-rectangular curves of CV and deviation from the normal triangular shape of the GCD curves. Overall, there is a limit up to which certain functional groups can contribute towards increasing the specific capacitance of an electrode and various competing factors determine the final output. That is why the excess of oxidized-N and overall oxygen atomic %age in SC electrode reduces its specific capacitance as compared to the other two materials. The presence of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N provide electroactive sites and boosts the specific capacitance [68], while the oxidized-N reduces the performance. Hence, as observed, the specific capacitance of SC@N-CQDs is more than double as compared to SC electrode.

Finally, the charge storage mechanism of SC@N-CQDs can be summarized from the following reaction mechanism and schematically described in Fig. 13. The SC@N-CQDs primarily store charge through EDL formation where the Na+ electrolyte ions adsorb on the surface of the anode and the PF6 - ions adsorb on the cathode. The surface functional groups and SC are involved in the faradic charge transfer reactions through the following equation:
SC@N-CQDs + x PF6 - ⇌ PF6x SC@N-CQDs + x e-
The Co3+ converts to Co2+ via exchange of an electron with PF6 - and vice versa in the reverse reaction. A similar reaction occurs with the N+ of the nitrogen functional groups.

Nitrogen rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are prepared hydrothermally using biowaste precursor, while the strontium cobaltite (SC) is prepared using sol-gel method. The two materials possess preferable electrochemical properties such as high conductivity of N-CQDs and high oxygen vacancies in SC. The concerted effects of the two materials are exhibited by the combined material, i.e., SC@N-CQDs, where it is observed that SC@N-CQDs possess a wide distribution of pore sizes, better conductivity than the individual materials, and demonstrate almost a double specific capacitance in the three-electrode configuration (386.5 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1). The practical two-electrode supercapacitor based on SC@N-CQDs gives a specific capacitance of 180.24 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1, an energy density of 7.51 Whkg−1, and a power density of 166.70 WKg−1. The device retains the specific capacitance by 80.8% after 5000 GCD cycles and successfully operated a red LED. The work demonstrates the successful conversion of biowaste into an energy source.

Rita Kumari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Study design, Writing manuscript, Poonam R. Kharangarh.: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing manuscript, Vinamrita Singh: Formal analysis, Investigation, Reviewing the manuscript, Ranjana Jha.: Reviewing the manuscript, Chhaya Ravi Kant: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision, Data interpretation, Reviewing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author (Rita Kumari) would like to express her sincere gratitude to Indira Gandhi Delhi Technical University for Women (IGDTUW), Delhi for providing the university Junior Research Fellowship for smooth ongoing research work.
